Ecotourism Tutorial

Joining occurrence, weather, and tourism data

Author

Your Name

🎯 Objectives

Practice joining relational datasets across occurrence, weather, and tourism tables, and interpret ecological patterns from the combined data.

🔧 Preparation

  • Install the ecotourism package from GitHub if you haven’t already:

    # install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("vahdatjavad/ecotourism")

  • Open your RStudio Project for this unit. Create a new .qmd document for today’s work.

  • Load the following libraries and datasets:

    library(ecotourism)
library(tidyverse)
data(manta_rays,        package = "ecotourism")
data(weather,           package = "ecotourism")
data(tourism_quarterly, package = "ecotourism")
data(tourism_region,    package = "ecotourism")

📥 Exercises

We will explore Manta Ray sightings in Queensland during March, the end of the wet season, when warm water temperatures draw manta rays to reef cleaning stations along the Great Barrier Reef.

The ecotourism package provides four linked datasets for this analysis:

Dataset Description
manta_rays Manta ray occurrence records from the Atlas of Living Australia (2014-2024)
weather Daily weather observations for the top weather stations near each organism
tourism_quarterly Quarterly domestic visitor counts for Australian tourism regions
tourism_region Australian tourism region names with centroid latitude and longitude

The key variables used for joining are:

Variable Dataset Description
ws_id manta_rays, weather, tourism_quarterly, tourism_region Weather station ID, the shared key linking all four tables
date manta_rays, weather Observation or measurement date (YYYY-MM-DD)
year manta_rays, tourism_quarterly Year of observation
quarter tourism_quarterly Quarter of the year (1 = Jan-Mar, 2 = Apr-Jun, ...)
month manta_rays Month of observation (integer, 1-12)
obs_lat manta_rays Latitude of the sighting
obs_lon manta_rays Longitude of the sighting
obs_state manta_rays Australian state of the sighting

Exercise 1

Filter manta_rays to sightings recorded in March in Queensland. Join this with the Q1 tourism data from tourism_quarterly. How many sightings have a matching tourism record? What proportion are missing, and what might explain the gaps?

SolutionSolution 1 
manta_march <- manta_rays |>
  filter(month == 3, obs_state == "Queensland")

manta_tourism <- manta_march |>
  left_join(
    tourism_quarterly |> filter(quarter == 1),
    by = c("ws_id", "year")
  )

manta_tourism |>
  summarise(
    total         = n(),
    missing_trips = sum(is.na(trips)),
    pct_missing   = round(mean(is.na(trips)) * 100, 1)
  )
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  total missing_trips pct_missing
  <int>         <int>       <dbl>
1    90            55        61.1

Gaps occur when a sighting’s nearest weather station (ws_id) does not appear in tourism_quarterly — not every station has a corresponding tourism region. A left_join retains all occurrence rows, with NA where no tourism record matched.

Exercise 2

Extend your joined dataset from Exercise 1 by bringing in the weather data. Join daily weather observations to your occurrence and tourism records. With all three tables now connected, does the number of sightings appear to vary with both weather conditions and tourism activity? Summarise and visualise what you find.

SolutionSolution 2 
manta_full <- manta_tourism |>
  left_join(
    weather |> select(ws_id, date, temp, prcp),
    by = c("ws_id", "date")
  )

manta_full |>
  filter( !is.na(prcp)) |>
  mutate(rainy = if_else(prcp > 5, "Rainy", "Dry")) |>
  group_by(rainy) |>
  summarise(
    n_sightings = n(),
    mean_trips  = mean(trips, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  rainy n_sightings mean_trips
  <chr>       <int>      <dbl>
1 Dry            33        NaN
2 Rainy           3        NaN
manta_full |>
  filter(!is.na(prcp)) |>
  group_by(date, prcp, trips) |>
  summarise(sightings = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = prcp, y = sightings)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#0072B2",size=3) +
  labs(
    x     = "Daily precipitation (mm)",
    y     = "Manta ray sightings",
    title = "Sightings vs rainfall, March Queensland"
  )

With all three tables joined, sighting frequency is shaped by both weather and tourism. Rainy days tend to suppress sightings, while higher tourism volume coincides with more recorded sightings, likely because more observers are in the water.

Exercise 3

Using the fully joined dataset from Exercise 2, compute the total March manta ray sightings and total tourist trips for Queensland per year. Is there a visible relationship between annual tourism volume and annual sighting frequency? Compute the correlation and discuss whether the relationship is causal, keeping in mind the role weather plays in driving both variables.

SolutionSolution 3 
annual <- manta_full |>
  filter(!is.na(trips) | !is.na(prcp)) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    sightings = n(),
    trips     = sum(trips, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

cor(annual$sightings, annual$trips, use = "complete.obs")
[1] 0.66
annual |>
  pivot_longer(c(sightings, trips), names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = value, colour = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~series, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Annual March sightings vs Q1 tourist trips (Queensland)",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

A positive correlation between sightings and trips is likely not directly causal in either direction. More tourists produce more observers and therefore more recorded sightings, inflating the occurrence count. But manta ray presence does not cause tourism. Both variables are more plausibly driven by a common factor: favourable weather years attract more tourists and also provide better marine conditions for sightings.

👌 Finishing up

Make sure you say thanks and good-bye to your tutor. Render both versions of your document with:

quarto render ecotourism_tutorial.qmd --to unilur-html
quarto render ecotourism_tutorial.qmd --to unilur-html+solution

Think about: how would your answers to Exercise 1 change if you used inner_join instead of left_join? Try it and see.
